turns.content: I'm back in the game after five years, but I'm confused about how imports work. What's the actual difference between std::prelude and core::prelude?

That is a great question to start with. The main difference is that the standard library prelude brings in common traits like Iterator, Into, and From for convenience, while the core prelude only provides fundamental types and traits from the core crate without any extra helpers. You might think you need both everywhere, but they serve different purposes depending on whether your project requires the full ecosystem or just a bare minimum.

You're saying I can just use std::prelude in every file? That sounds like it would make my code cleaner than manually importing everything. I bet that's why it became the default pattern for all new projects.

Actually, you've got the concept backwards on that point. The prelude is designed to be optional so you can write minimal no_std code without getting in its way. In fact, many crates like the embedded ecosystem avoid it entirely because they want to ensure every file has consistent imports without relying on the default behavior.
So if I'm building a system that needs to be extremely lightweight and doesn't use any standard library features, should I even import from prelude at all? Is there a better way?

Yes, for no_std environments like microcontrollers you typically don't want the prelude because it assumes certain traits are available. Instead of importing blindly, you should create your own custom module in your crate root to define exactly which types and traits you need, giving you full control over what gets exposed globally.
